text: Given an array of integers, find the maximum and minimum element in a single traversal.
expected_approach: Traverse once keeping track of max and min variables. Initialize both to arr[0]. Update max if current > max, update min if current < min. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Given an array, move all zeros to the end while maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements.
expected_approach: Use two pointers: left pointer tracks position to place next non-zero. Traverse with right pointer; when non-zero found, place it at left and increment left. Fill rest with zeros. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Find the contiguous subarray with the maximum sum (Kadane's algorithm).
expected_approach: Track current_sum and max_sum. For each element, current_sum = max(element, current_sum + element). Update max_sum = max(max_sum, current_sum). This handles negative numbers correctly. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Given an array of n integers where each element is between 1 and n, find all duplicates without extra space.
expected_approach: For each element, go to index abs(arr[i])-1 and negate the value there. If value is already negative, abs(arr[i]) is a duplicate. Restore signs if needed. O(n) time, O(1) space using the array itself as a hash map.

text: Given an unsorted array, find the length of the longest consecutive sequence.
expected_approach: Put all elements in a hash set. For each element, check if element-1 is NOT in set (start of sequence). If so, count consecutive elements by checking element+1, element+2, etc. O(n) average time.

text: Given an array, find the next permutation in lexicographic order in-place.
expected_approach: Find largest index i where arr[i] < arr[i+1] (from right). Find largest index j > i where arr[j] > arr[i]. Swap arr[i] and arr[j]. Reverse the suffix starting at arr[i+1]. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Check if two strings are anagrams of each other.
expected_approach: Sort both strings and compare, OR use a frequency count array/hashmap of 26 chars. Count chars in first string incrementing, count in second decrementing. Check all zeros at end. O(n) time.

text: Find the longest palindromic substring in a given string.
expected_approach: Expand around center approach: for each character (and gap between characters), expand outward while chars match. Track max length palindrome found. O(n^2) time, O(1) space. Alternatively Manacher's algorithm for O(n).

text: Implement strstr() — find the first occurrence of a substring (needle) in a string (haystack).
expected_approach: KMP algorithm: build failure function (partial match table) for needle in O(m). Use it to scan haystack in O(n) without backtracking. Total O(n+m). Naive approach is O(nm) but mention KMP for full marks.

text: Given a string, find the minimum window substring that contains all characters of a target string.
expected_approach: Sliding window with two hashmaps: one for target frequencies, one for current window. Expand right pointer; when all target chars satisfied, shrink left pointer. Track minimum window. O(n) time.

text: Given a list of words, group all anagrams together.
expected_approach: Use a hashmap where key is the sorted version of each word (or a frequency tuple). Group words by their key. O(n * k log k) where k is max word length.

text: Find the longest substring without repeating characters.
expected_approach: Sliding window with a hashmap storing last seen index of each character. Maintain left pointer; when duplicate found, move left to max(left, last_seen[char]+1). Track max window size. O(n) time.

text: Find GCD of two numbers efficiently.
expected_approach: Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a%b), base case gcd(a,0)=a. Runs in O(log(min(a,b))) time. LCM = a*b/gcd(a,b).

text: Check if a number is prime and generate all primes up to N.
expected_approach: For single number: check divisors up to sqrt(n). For all primes up to N: Sieve of Eratosthenes — mark multiples of each prime starting from 2. O(N log log N) time, O(N) space.

text: Compute a^b mod m efficiently.
expected_approach: Binary exponentiation (fast power): if b is even, result = (a^(b/2))^2 mod m. If odd, result = a * a^(b-1) mod m. O(log b) time. Essential for large number computations under modulo.

text: Given n, count numbers from 1 to n that are coprime to n (Euler's Totient Function).
expected_approach: Euler's totient: phi(n) = n * product of (1 - 1/p) for each prime factor p of n. Factorize n, for each prime factor p: phi = phi - phi/p. Result gives count of integers coprime to n. O(sqrt(n)) time.

text: Find the single non-duplicate element in an array where every other element appears exactly twice.
expected_approach: XOR all elements together. Since a XOR a = 0 and a XOR 0 = a, all pairs cancel out leaving only the unique element. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Count the number of set bits (1s) in the binary representation of a number (popcount).
expected_approach: Brian Kernighan's algorithm: repeatedly do n = n & (n-1) which clears the lowest set bit, counting iterations until n=0. O(number of set bits). Alternative: use built-in __builtin_popcount or bit_count().

text: Find two non-repeating elements in an array where all others appear twice.
expected_approach: XOR all elements to get xor of the two unique numbers. Find any set bit in this XOR (use rightmost: xor & -xor). Partition array into two groups by this bit, XOR each group separately to isolate the two numbers. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Given an integer, generate all possible subsets of a set with n elements using bitmask.
expected_approach: Iterate i from 0 to 2^n - 1. For each i, the j-th bit being set means element j is in the subset. Use (i >> j) & 1 to check. Total 2^n subsets, O(n * 2^n) time to enumerate all.

text: Given an array and multiple range sum queries [l, r], answer each in O(1) after preprocessing.
expected_approach: Build prefix sum array where prefix[i] = sum of arr[0..i-1]. Answer query [l,r] as prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]. O(n) preprocessing, O(1) per query.

text: Find the number of subarrays with sum equal to k.
expected_approach: Use prefix sum with a hashmap. At each index, current prefix sum minus k gives the prefix sum we need to have seen before. Count occurrences using hashmap. O(n) time, O(n) space.

text: Given a 2D matrix, answer range sum queries for submatrices in O(1).
expected_approach: Build 2D prefix sum: prefix[i][j] = sum of all elements in rectangle from (0,0) to (i-1,j-1). Query (r1,c1,r2,c2) = prefix[r2+1][c2+1] - prefix[r1][c2+1] - prefix[r2+1][c1] + prefix[r1][c1]. Inclusion-exclusion principle.

text: Find the maximum sum subarray of size exactly k.
expected_approach: Compute prefix sum array. For each ending index i >= k, subarray sum = prefix[i+1] - prefix[i-k+1]. Slide this window across the array keeping track of max. O(n) time after O(n) prefix sum build.

text: Given a sorted array, find if there exists a pair with a given sum.
expected_approach: Place left pointer at start, right pointer at end. If sum equals target, found. If sum < target, move left right. If sum > target, move right left. O(n) time, O(1) space. Only works on sorted arrays.

text: Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in-place and return the new length.
expected_approach: Left pointer marks position of last unique element. Right pointer scans ahead. When arr[right] != arr[left], increment left and copy arr[right] to arr[left]. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero (3Sum problem).
expected_approach: Sort the array. Fix first element with outer loop (skip duplicates). Use two pointers on remaining subarray to find pairs summing to -first. Skip duplicates for both pointers. O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space.

text: Given n non-negative integers representing heights of bars, find the container that holds the most water.
expected_approach: Two pointers at both ends. Area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right - left). Move the pointer with smaller height inward (it can't contribute more by moving the larger one). O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Find the maximum sum of a subarray of size exactly k.
expected_approach: Compute sum of first k elements. Slide window right: add new element, remove leftmost element, update max. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Find the length of the longest subarray with sum at most k (all positive elements).
expected_approach: Expand right pointer adding elements. When sum exceeds k, shrink left pointer until sum <= k. Track max window size at each step. Works in O(n) for positive elements.

text: Find the minimum length subarray with sum greater than or equal to k (positive elements).
expected_approach: Expand right, when sum >= k record window size and shrink from left trying to minimize. Since all elements positive, left pointer never needs to go back. O(n) time.

text: Find the number of subarrays with exactly k distinct integers.
expected_approach: Use the identity: exactly k = at most k minus at most k-1. Write helper function for at most k distinct using sliding window with a frequency map. Call it twice. O(n) time.

text: Implement binary search on a sorted array to find target index, return -1 if not found.
expected_approach: Set low=0, high=n-1. While low<=high: mid=(low+high)/2. If arr[mid]==target return mid. If arr[mid]<target set low=mid+1, else high=mid-1. O(log n) time.

text: Find the first and last position of a target element in a sorted array.
expected_approach: Run binary search twice: once finding leftmost occurrence (when found, record and set high=mid-1), once for rightmost (when found, record and set low=mid+1). O(log n) time.

text: Search in a rotated sorted array where you don't know the rotation point.
expected_approach: Binary search: find mid. Check which half is sorted (compare arr[low] with arr[mid]). If target lies in sorted half, search there; else search the other half. O(log n) time.

text: Find the minimum element in a rotated sorted array with no duplicates.
expected_approach: Binary search: if arr[mid] > arr[right], minimum is in right half (set low=mid+1). Else minimum is in left half including mid (set high=mid). When low==high, that's the minimum. O(log n).

text: Given a sorted matrix where each row and column is sorted, search for a target.
expected_approach: Start from top-right corner. If current == target, found. If current > target, move left. If current < target, move down. Each step eliminates a row or column. O(m+n) time.

text: Find the kth smallest element in a sorted matrix.
expected_approach: Binary search on the answer value (not index). For a mid value, count elements <= mid using the top-right traversal trick. Binary search until low==high. O(n log(max-min)) time.

text: Implement merge sort and explain its time and space complexity.
expected_approach: Divide array in half recursively until single elements. Merge two sorted halves by comparing elements and placing smaller one first. O(n log n) time always. O(n) auxiliary space for merge step. Stable sort.

text: Implement quicksort and explain average vs worst case.
expected_approach: Choose pivot (ideally random or median-of-three). Partition: elements < pivot left, > pivot right. Recurse on both halves. Average O(n log n) with O(log n) stack space. Worst case O(n^2) with bad pivot (already sorted). Randomization avoids worst case.

text: Sort an array of 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass without extra space (Dutch National Flag).
expected_approach: Three pointers: low (boundary of 0s), mid (current element), high (boundary of 2s). If arr[mid]==0, swap with low, increment both. If 1, just increment mid. If 2, swap with high, decrement high (don't increment mid since swapped element unchecked). O(n) time.

text: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals.
expected_approach: Sort intervals by start time. Initialize result with first interval. For each subsequent interval, if it overlaps with last in result (start <= last.end), merge by updating last.end = max(last.end, current.end). Else append. O(n log n).

text: Find the kth largest element in an unsorted array efficiently.
expected_approach: Quickselect algorithm: partition like quicksort. If pivot lands at position k (from end), return it. If k is in right partition recurse right, else recurse left. Average O(n), worst O(n^2). Alternatively use min-heap of size k: O(n log k).

text: Find the first non-repeating character in a string.
expected_approach: Two passes: first pass build frequency map of all characters. Second pass find first character with frequency 1. O(n) time, O(1) space (only 26 chars).

text: Given an array of integers and a target sum, find all pairs that sum to the target.
expected_approach: Use a hash set. For each element, check if (target - element) is in set. If yes, found a pair. Add element to set. O(n) time, O(n) space. Handle duplicates carefully.

text: Given a list of transactions, find the person who spent the most in each category.
expected_approach: Use nested hashmaps: outer key is category, inner key is person, value is total spend. Iterate transactions updating inner map. Then for each category find max. O(n) time where n is number of transactions.

text: Design a data structure that supports insert, delete, and getRandom in O(1) average time.
expected_approach: Combine hashmap and array. Hashmap stores element->index. Array stores elements. Insert: append to array, add to map. Delete: swap with last element, update map, pop last. GetRandom: return arr[random index]. All O(1) average.

text: Find the longest subarray with equal number of 0s and 1s.
expected_approach: Replace 0s with -1s. Find longest subarray with sum 0. Use prefix sum + hashmap: store first occurrence of each prefix sum. When same prefix sum seen again, length is current_index - first_occurrence. O(n) time.

text: Implement recursive binary search and analyze its recurrence relation.
expected_approach: Base case: low > high (not found) or arr[mid]==target. Recursive case: search left or right half. Recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1). By Master theorem: O(log n). Stack depth O(log n). Iterative version uses O(1) space.

text: Generate all permutations of a string or array.
expected_approach: Fix each element at current position by swapping it with elements from current to end. Recurse on remaining. Backtrack by swapping back. n! permutations total, O(n * n!) time to generate all. Use visited array to handle duplicates.

text: Generate all subsets (power set) of a given set.
expected_approach: At each index, two choices: include or exclude current element. Recurse with both choices. 2^n subsets total. Base case: when index == n, add current subset to result. O(n * 2^n) time.

text: Solve Tower of Hanoi for n disks and explain the minimum number of moves.
expected_approach: Move n-1 disks from source to auxiliary using target. Move nth disk from source to target. Move n-1 disks from auxiliary to target using source. Recurrence T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1, solution is 2^n - 1 moves. O(2^n) time.

text: Reverse a singly linked list iteratively.
expected_approach: Three pointers: prev=null, current=head, next=null. In each iteration: save next=current.next, point current.next=prev, advance prev=current, current=next. When current is null, prev is new head. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Detect if a linked list has a cycle and find the start of the cycle.
expected_approach: Floyd's algorithm: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If they meet, cycle exists. To find start: reset slow to head, keep fast at meeting point, both move 1 step at a time — they meet at cycle start. Mathematical proof based on distances. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted linked list.
expected_approach: Use dummy head node. Compare heads of both lists, attach smaller to result, advance that list's pointer. When one list exhausted, append remaining other list. O(m+n) time, O(1) space (no new nodes created except dummy).

text: Find the middle of a linked list in one pass.
expected_approach: Slow pointer moves 1 step, fast pointer moves 2 steps. When fast reaches end (null or null.next), slow is at middle. For even length lists, slow lands at second middle. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Given a linked list, reverse nodes in groups of k.
expected_approach: Reverse first k nodes iteratively. Connect reversed group's tail to result of recursively processing remaining list. Handle case where fewer than k nodes remain (leave as-is). O(n) time, O(n/k) recursive stack space.

text: Merge k sorted linked lists efficiently.
expected_approach: Use a min-heap of size k containing the head of each list. Extract min, add to result, push that node's next into heap. O(n log k) time where n is total nodes. Alternative: divide and conquer merge pairs in O(n log k).

text: Implement a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin in O(1) time.
expected_approach: Maintain two stacks: main stack and min stack. On push, push to main; push to min stack only if new value <= current min. On pop, if popped value == min stack top, pop min stack too. getMin returns min stack top. O(1) all operations.

text: Check if a string of brackets is valid (balanced parentheses).
expected_approach: Push opening brackets onto stack. When closing bracket encountered, check if stack top is matching opener — if not, invalid. At end, stack must be empty. O(n) time, O(n) space.

text: Evaluate a Reverse Polish Notation (postfix) expression.
expected_approach: Iterate tokens. If number, push to stack. If operator, pop two operands (note order for - and /), apply operator, push result. Final stack top is answer. O(n) time, O(n) space.

text: Design a stack that supports push, pop, and retrieving the maximum element in O(1).
expected_approach: Similar to min-stack: maintain a parallel max-stack. Push to max-stack when new value >= current max. Pop from max-stack when popped value == max-stack top. O(1) all operations, O(n) space.

text: Implement a queue using two stacks.
expected_approach: Stack1 for enqueue, Stack2 for dequeue. Enqueue: push to Stack1. Dequeue: if Stack2 empty, pour all of Stack1 into Stack2, then pop from Stack2. Amortized O(1) per operation since each element moves at most twice.

text: Given a stream of integers, find the median after each insertion.
expected_approach: Use two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half. Maintain size balance (differ by at most 1). Median is top of larger heap or average of both tops. Insert to max-heap first, then rebalance. O(log n) insert, O(1) median.

text: Implement a circular queue (ring buffer) with fixed size.
expected_approach: Use array with head and tail pointers and count. Enqueue: arr[tail] = val, tail = (tail+1)%size, count++. Dequeue: val = arr[head], head = (head+1)%size, count--. isFull: count==size, isEmpty: count==0. O(1) all operations.

text: Given a list of tasks with cooldown period n, find minimum time to finish all tasks.
expected_approach: Count task frequencies. Most frequent task determines the frame: (max_freq-1)*(n+1) + count_of_tasks_with_max_freq. Answer is max of this and total number of tasks (in case tasks outnumber idle slots). O(number of unique tasks) time.

text: Find the next greater element for each element in an array.
expected_approach: Maintain a decreasing monotonic stack of indices. For each element, while stack not empty and current > arr[stack.top()], pop and set next_greater[popped] = current. Push current index. Remaining elements in stack have no next greater (set -1). O(n) time.

text: Calculate the amount of rainwater trapped between bars of given heights.
expected_approach: For each bar, water = min(max_left, max_right) - height. Precompute left_max and right_max arrays. O(n) time, O(n) space. Alternatively use two pointers: move pointer with smaller height, water at that bar determined by min of both maxes. O(1) space.

text: Find the largest rectangle in a histogram.
expected_approach: Monotonic increasing stack: push bar indices. When current bar is shorter than stack top, pop and calculate area: width = current_index - stack_top_after_pop - 1, height = popped bar height. Process all remaining bars at end. O(n) time.

text: Find the maximum rectangle of 1s in a binary matrix.
expected_approach: Reduce to largest rectangle in histogram. Build histogram row by row: if cell is 1, height[col]++; if 0, height[col]=0. Apply histogram solution on each row's heights. O(m*n) time total.

text: Find the kth largest element in a stream of numbers.
expected_approach: Maintain a min-heap of size k. For each new number: if heap size < k, push. Else if number > heap top, pop and push new number. The heap top is always the kth largest. O(n log k) total time.

text: Merge k sorted arrays into one sorted array.
expected_approach: Push the first element of each array into a min-heap with (value, array_index, element_index). Extract min, add to result, push next element from same array. O(n log k) time where n is total elements.

text: Find the top k frequent elements in an array.
expected_approach: Build frequency map O(n). Use min-heap of size k: if heap size < k push, else if current freq > heap top freq, replace. Or use bucket sort: bucket[freq] = list of elements, scan from high freq to low. O(n log k) heap approach, O(n) bucket sort.

text: Schedule jobs to minimize total weighted completion time.
expected_approach: Greedy with heap: process jobs in order of ratio weight/processing_time (higher ratio first). This minimizes sum of weighted completion times. Sort by ratio and compute cumulative completion times. Proof by exchange argument. O(n log n).

text: Implement all three DFS traversals of a binary tree: inorder, preorder, and postorder.
expected_approach: Inorder: left, root, right. Preorder: root, left, right. Postorder: left, right, root. All recursive with O(n) time, O(h) space where h is height. Iterative versions use explicit stack. Inorder of BST gives sorted output.

text: Find the height (maximum depth) of a binary tree.
expected_approach: Recursive: height = 1 + max(height(left), height(right)). Base case: null node returns 0. O(n) time, O(h) space. Iterative BFS: level-order traversal counting levels. O(n) time, O(width) space.

text: Check if a binary tree is balanced (height difference of subtrees <= 1 for all nodes).
expected_approach: Post-order traversal returning height. If subtree is unbalanced return -1 (sentinel). At each node: get left and right heights, if either is -1 or |left-right| > 1, return -1. Else return 1+max(left,right). O(n) time, O(h) space.

text: Find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two nodes in a binary tree.
expected_approach: Recursive: if root is null or equals p or q, return root. Get LCA from left and right subtrees. If both return non-null, current root is LCA. If only one side returns non-null, that's the LCA. O(n) time, O(h) space.

text: Serialize and deserialize a binary tree.
expected_approach: Serialize: preorder traversal with null markers (e.g., '#'). Deserialize: use a queue/iterator of values; if value is '#' return null, else create node and recurse for left and right children. O(n) time and space.

text: Find the maximum path sum in a binary tree where path can start and end at any node.
expected_approach: Post-order DFS: at each node compute max gain from left (max(0, gain(left))) and right. Update global_max with node.val + left_gain + right_gain. Return node.val + max(left_gain, right_gain) to parent (single path). O(n) time.

text: Insert a node into a BST and search for a value.
expected_approach: Search: if root null return null/false. If target < root go left, if > go right, if == found. Insert: same traversal but when null position found, create new node there. O(h) time where h is height. O(log n) average, O(n) worst case.

text: Delete a node from a BST.
expected_approach: Find node. Three cases: (1) no children: just remove. (2) one child: replace with child. (3) two children: find inorder successor (leftmost in right subtree), copy its value to current node, delete the inorder successor. O(h) time.

text: Validate whether a given binary tree is a valid BST.
expected_approach: Pass min and max bounds during traversal. Root has (-inf, +inf). Left child gets (min, root.val). Right child gets (root.val, max). At each node check min < val < max. O(n) time, O(h) space.

text: Find the kth smallest element in a BST.
expected_approach: Inorder traversal (left, root, right) of BST gives sorted order. Count nodes visited; return when count == k. Iterative inorder with stack for O(h) space. O(h+k) time. Morris traversal for O(1) space.

text: Convert a sorted array to a height-balanced BST.
expected_approach: Recursively pick middle element as root. Left subarray becomes left subtree, right subarray becomes right subtree. This ensures balance since each split is roughly half. O(n) time, O(log n) stack space.

text: Implement a Trie with insert, search, and startsWith operations.
expected_approach: Each node has children array of 26 (or hashmap for generic) and isEnd flag. Insert: traverse/create nodes for each char, mark last as isEnd. Search: traverse, return isEnd of last node. StartsWith: traverse, return true if all chars found. O(L) per operation, O(L*alphabet) space.

text: Given a list of words, find the longest word that can be built character by character from other words in the list.
expected_approach: Build trie from all words. BFS/DFS through trie: only visit nodes where isEnd==true (meaning prefix is a valid word). Track longest word found. O(sum of word lengths) time and space.

text: Find the maximum XOR of two numbers in an array.
expected_approach: Build binary trie from all numbers (bit by bit from MSB). For each number, traverse trie greedily choosing opposite bit at each level to maximize XOR. O(32*n) = O(n) time and space.

text: Design a search autocomplete system that returns top 3 historical sentences by frequency for a given prefix.
expected_approach: Trie where each node stores a map of sentence->frequency. On input, traverse to current prefix node, return top 3 from that node's map (or DFS to collect all). Update frequencies on '#' (end of input). O(prefix_length) search time.

text: Build a segment tree for range sum queries with point updates.
expected_approach: Build: recursively split range, leaf nodes hold array values, internal nodes hold sum of children. O(n) build time, O(n) space. Update: traverse to leaf, update, recalculate sums on way back. O(log n). Query [l,r]: split into O(log n) segments. O(log n) query.

text: Implement range minimum query (RMQ) using sparse table for static arrays.
expected_approach: Precompute sparse table: st[i][j] = minimum of 2^j elements starting at i. O(n log n) preprocessing. Query [l,r]: k = floor(log2(r-l+1)), answer = min(st[l][k], st[r-2^k+1][k]). O(1) query, O(n log n) space.

text: Implement a segment tree with lazy propagation for range updates and range queries.
expected_approach: Add lazy array to segment tree. Range update: if node range fully in update range, apply update and mark lazy. Otherwise push lazy to children, then update. Query: push lazy before recursing. O(log n) both operations. Lazy propagation avoids redundant updates.

text: Implement a Fenwick tree (Binary Indexed Tree) for prefix sum queries and point updates.
expected_approach: Update index i: add value to BIT[i], then i += i & (-i) (add lowest set bit), repeat. Query prefix sum [1,i]: sum BIT[i], then i -= i & (-i) (remove lowest set bit), repeat. O(log n) both operations. O(n) space.

text: Count inversions in an array using a Fenwick tree or merge sort.
expected_approach: Merge sort: during merge step, when right element is placed before left elements, those left elements are all inversions. Count them. O(n log n). Alternatively: coordinate compress, process right to left, for each element query BIT for count of smaller elements seen so far. O(n log n).

text: Implement BFS and DFS on an adjacency list graph. Find if path exists between two nodes.
expected_approach: BFS: use queue, visited set. Enqueue source, mark visited. Dequeue, check if destination, enqueue unvisited neighbors. DFS: use stack or recursion, mark visited, explore each unvisited neighbor. Both O(V+E) time and space.

text: Count the number of connected components in an undirected graph.
expected_approach: Iterate all vertices. For each unvisited vertex, run BFS/DFS marking all reachable vertices as visited — this is one component. Increment counter. O(V+E) time. Alternatively use DSU: union edges, count distinct roots.

text: Detect a cycle in an undirected graph.
expected_approach: DFS with parent tracking: when visiting neighbors, if neighbor is visited and not parent, cycle exists. For BFS: if neighbor already visited during BFS traversal from same component, cycle exists. O(V+E) time.

text: Detect a cycle in a directed graph.
expected_approach: DFS with three states: unvisited (0), in current path (1), fully processed (2). If we reach a node with state 1, cycle exists. If state 2, already processed safely. Reset state to 2 when backtracking. O(V+E) time.

text: Find all bridges in an undirected graph (edges whose removal disconnects the graph).
expected_approach: Tarjan's bridge finding: DFS maintaining discovery time and low value (lowest discovery reachable). Edge (u,v) is bridge if low[v] > disc[u] (v can't reach u or ancestors without this edge). O(V+E) time.

text: Given a grid of 0s and 1s, count the number of islands (connected components of 1s).
expected_approach: For each unvisited '1' cell, run BFS/DFS marking all connected '1' cells as visited (flip to '0' or use visited array). Each BFS/DFS call = one island. O(m*n) time and space.

text: Find shortest path in an unweighted graph from source to all vertices.
expected_approach: BFS from source. Distance array initialized to -1. When first visiting a node, set dist[node] = dist[parent] + 1. BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs because it explores level by level. O(V+E) time.

text: Find shortest paths from a single source in a weighted graph with non-negative edges (Dijkstra's).
expected_approach: Min-heap of (distance, node). Initialize dist[source]=0, all others infinity. Extract min, relax neighbors: if dist[u]+weight < dist[v], update dist[v] and push to heap. Skip if already processed with shorter distance. O((V+E) log V) with binary heap.

text: Find shortest paths with negative edges (Bellman-Ford) and detect negative cycles.
expected_approach: Relax all edges V-1 times. After V-1 iterations, all shortest paths (without negative cycles) are found. Run one more iteration: if any distance still decreases, negative cycle exists. O(VE) time. Works with negative edges, unlike Dijkstra.

text: Find shortest paths between all pairs of vertices (Floyd-Warshall).
expected_approach: dp[i][j][k] = shortest path from i to j using only vertices 0..k as intermediates. Reduce to 2D: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]) for each intermediate k. O(V^3) time, O(V^2) space. Detects negative cycles if dp[i][i] < 0.

text: Find the Minimum Spanning Tree using Kruskal's algorithm.
expected_approach: Sort all edges by weight. Process edges in order: if adding edge doesn't create cycle (check using DSU/Union-Find), add to MST. Stop after V-1 edges added. O(E log E) for sorting, O(E alpha(V)) for DSU operations. Total O(E log E).

text: Find the Minimum Spanning Tree using Prim's algorithm.
expected_approach: Start from any vertex. Use min-heap of (weight, vertex). Greedily add minimum weight edge that connects visited vertex to unvisited vertex. Mark added vertices as visited. O(E log V) with binary heap, O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap.

text: Implement Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) with path compression and union by rank.
expected_approach: Find: follow parent pointers to root, compress path by making all nodes point directly to root. Union: find both roots, attach smaller rank tree under larger rank root. With both optimizations: nearly O(1) amortized per operation (O(alpha(n)) inverse Ackermann).

text: Determine if adding each edge creates a cycle (used in Kruskal's).
expected_approach: For each edge (u,v): find roots of u and v. If same root, adding this edge creates a cycle. If different roots, union them (no cycle). DSU with path compression and union by rank gives O(alpha(n)) per operation.

text: Find the number of provinces (groups of directly/indirectly connected cities) in a connection matrix.
expected_approach: Initialize each city as its own component. Union cities that are directly connected. Count number of distinct roots (components). O(n^2 * alpha(n)) time processing the matrix.

text: Given a list of accounts with emails, merge accounts that share any email.
expected_approach: Map each email to account index using hashmap. DSU on account indices: when same email found in multiple accounts, union those accounts. Group accounts by root. Sort emails within each group. O(n log n) for sorting.

text: Find a valid topological ordering of tasks given their dependencies.
expected_approach: Kahn's algorithm (BFS): compute in-degrees. Queue all nodes with in-degree 0. Process queue: add to result, reduce in-degree of neighbors, add newly zero in-degree nodes to queue. If result length < V, cycle exists. O(V+E) time.

text: Determine if a course schedule is possible given prerequisites (cycle detection in directed graph).
expected_approach: Build directed graph of prerequisites. Run topological sort (Kahn's or DFS). If all courses appear in topological order, schedule is possible. If cycle detected (not all nodes processed), impossible. O(V+E) time.

text: Find the shortest path in a DAG efficiently.
expected_approach: Topological sort the DAG. Process vertices in topological order: for each vertex u, relax all outgoing edges. Since topological order respects dependencies, each edge processed exactly once. O(V+E) time, works with negative weights in DAGs.

text: Given a dictionary of alien language words in sorted order, find the character ordering.
expected_approach: Compare adjacent words to find character ordering constraints (first differing position gives an edge). Build graph. Topological sort gives the alien alphabet order. If cycle exists, order is invalid. If word1 is prefix of word2 and appears after, invalid. O(total chars) time.

text: Find all strongly connected components in a directed graph (Kosaraju's algorithm).
expected_approach: DFS on original graph, push vertices to stack in finish-time order. Transpose graph (reverse all edges). Pop from stack, run DFS on transposed graph — each DFS tree is an SCC. O(V+E) time, O(V+E) space. Two passes of DFS.

text: Find all SCCs using Tarjan's algorithm in one pass.
expected_approach: DFS maintaining discovery time and low-link values. Use a stack of visited nodes. Node u is SCC root if low[u] == disc[u]. Pop stack until u is popped — these form one SCC. O(V+E) time, single DFS pass. More cache-friendly than Kosaraju's.

text: Solve the N-Queens problem: place N queens on NxN board so no two attack each other.
expected_approach: Place queens row by row. For each column in current row, check if placement is safe (no conflict in column, left diagonal, right diagonal — track with sets). Place queen, recurse to next row, backtrack by removing. O(N!) time in worst case but pruning reduces this significantly.

text: Find all combinations that sum to a target (elements can be reused).
expected_approach: Backtracking: at each step, choose element >= last chosen (to avoid duplicates), subtract from target, recurse. Base case: target==0 (found solution) or target<0 (prune). O(N^(T/M)) where T is target and M is smallest element.

text: Solve Sudoku: fill a 9x9 grid with digits 1-9 following standard rules.
expected_approach: Find empty cell, try digits 1-9. For each digit, check if valid (not in same row, column, or 3x3 box — use sets/bitmask). If valid, place and recurse. If recursion fails, backtrack (remove digit). O(9^empty_cells) worst case, but constraint propagation prunes heavily.

text: Generate all valid combinations of n pairs of parentheses.
expected_approach: Track open and close counts. Add '(' if open < n. Add ')' if close < open. Backtrack both choices. When string length == 2n, valid combination found. O(4^n / sqrt(n)) time (Catalan number of valid sequences).

text: Find the maximum subarray sum using divide and conquer.
expected_approach: Divide array in half. Max subarray is either entirely in left half, right half, or crosses midpoint. Crossing case: find max suffix of left half + max prefix of right half. Recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), solution O(n log n). Kadane's is O(n) but this demonstrates D&C.

text: Implement merge sort and use it to count inversions in an array.
expected_approach: During merge step: when element from right array is merged before elements remaining in left array, those left elements are all inversions with the right element. Add count of remaining left elements. O(n log n) time.

text: Find the closest pair of points in a plane efficiently.
expected_approach: Sort points by x. Divide into halves. Recursively find min distance d in each half. Check strip of width 2d around midline — only need to check at most 7 points ahead for each point in strip (geometric argument). O(n log n) time.

text: Multiply two large numbers efficiently (Karatsuba algorithm).
expected_approach: Split each number into halves: x = x1*B + x0, y = y1*B + y0. Karatsuba: compute z2=x1*y1, z0=x0*y0, z1=(x1+x0)*(y1+y0)-z2-z0. Result = z2*B^2 + z1*B + z0. Recurrence T(n)=3T(n/2)+O(n), solution O(n^1.585). Beats O(n^2) naive.

text: Given an array of coin denominations and a target, find minimum coins to make the target (assuming standard denominations that work greedily).
expected_approach: Sort denominations descending. Greedily take as many of largest denomination as possible without exceeding target, then move to next. Note: greedy works for standard coin systems (e.g., US coins) but not all — DP needed for arbitrary denominations.

text: Given activities with start and end times, select maximum number of non-overlapping activities.
expected_approach: Sort activities by end time. Greedily select activity if its start time >= end time of last selected activity. Exchange argument proof: selecting earliest-finishing activity never loses an option. O(n log n) for sort, O(n) selection.

text: Given an array, find minimum number of jumps to reach the last index (Jump Game II).
expected_approach: Greedy: track current reach and next reach. At each position update next reach = max(next_reach, i+nums[i]). When we reach current reach boundary, increment jumps and update current reach to next reach. O(n) time.

text: Assign n tasks to n workers minimizing total time given each worker has different speeds.
expected_approach: Sort tasks ascending, sort workers by speed descending. Pair fastest worker with hardest task, second fastest with second hardest, etc. Proof by exchange argument: any other assignment takes at least as long. O(n log n) for sorting.

text: Given gas stations in a circle, find starting station where you can complete the circuit.
expected_approach: If total gas >= total cost, solution exists. Find starting point: track running surplus. Whenever surplus goes negative, reset start to next station and reset surplus. The last valid start is the answer. O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Find the nth Fibonacci number using dynamic programming (bottom-up and top-down).
expected_approach: Top-down: memoize recursive calls in array/hashmap. Bottom-up: dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1, dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. O(n) time, O(n) space. Optimize to O(1) space by only keeping last two values. Matrix exponentiation gives O(log n).

text: Find the length of the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS).
expected_approach: DP: dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. For each i, check all j<i where arr[j]<arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[j]+1). O(n^2). Optimal: patience sorting with binary search, O(n log n) — maintain array of smallest tail elements for each LIS length.

text: 0/1 Knapsack: given weights and values, maximize value within weight capacity.
expected_approach: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]) if weight[i] <= w. O(n*W) time and space. Optimize to O(W) space by using 1D dp array traversed right to left.

text: Coin change: find minimum number of coins to make a target amount.
expected_approach: dp[0]=0, dp[i] = min coins to make amount i. For each amount, try all coins: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1) if i>=coin. Initialize dp with infinity. O(amount * num_coins) time, O(amount) space. Unlike greedy, works for any coin system.

text: Given a rod of length n and prices for each length, find maximum revenue from cutting the rod.
expected_approach: dp[i] = max revenue from rod of length i. For each length i, try all first cuts j: dp[i] = max(dp[i-j] + price[j]). O(n^2) time, O(n) space. This is unbounded knapsack — each cut size can be used multiple times.

text: Count the number of ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time.
expected_approach: dp[i] = number of ways to reach step i. dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] (from one step below or two steps below). Base cases dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2. O(n) time, O(1) space with two variables. Generalizes to k steps.

text: Find minimum path sum from top-left to bottom-right in a grid (can only move right or down).
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = minimum cost to reach cell (i,j). dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Handle first row and column separately (only one way to reach them). O(m*n) time and space. Optimize to O(n) space with 1D dp.

text: Count unique paths from top-left to bottom-right in a grid with obstacles.
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = number of paths to (i,j). If obstacle, dp[i][j]=0. Else dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Handle first row/column: 0 paths if obstacle encountered. O(m*n) time and space.

text: Find maximum side length of a square of 1s in a binary matrix.
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = side length of largest square with bottom-right corner at (i,j). If matrix[i][j]==1: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]). Answer is max dp value. O(m*n) time and space.

text: Given a matrix, find the maximum sum rectangle.
expected_approach: Fix left and right column boundaries. For each pair, compute row sums between columns. Apply Kadane's on these row sums to find max sum subarray. O(n^2 * m) time. This reduces the 2D problem to 1D Kadane's for each column pair.

text: Find the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) of two strings.
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = LCS length of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1]. If s1[i-1]==s2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). O(m*n) time and space. Can reconstruct the subsequence by backtracking.

text: Find the edit distance (Levenshtein distance) between two strings.
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = min operations to convert s1[0..i] to s2[0..j]. If chars equal: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: min of insert(dp[i][j-1]+1), delete(dp[i-1][j]+1), replace(dp[i-1][j-1]+1). O(m*n) time and space.

text: Check if a string is an interleaving of two other strings.
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = true if s3[0..i+j-1] is interleaving of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1]. dp[i][j] = (dp[i-1][j] && s1[i-1]==s3[i+j-1]) || (dp[i][j-1] && s2[j-1]==s3[i+j-1]). O(m*n) time and space.

text: Find the minimum number of cuts to make all substrings of a string palindromes.
expected_approach: Precompute palindrome table: isPalin[i][j] in O(n^2). Then dp[i] = min cuts for s[0..i]. dp[i] = min(dp[j-1]+1) for all j<=i where s[j..i] is palindrome. O(n^2) time. Can reduce to O(n) with Manacher's for palindrome check.

text: Find the number of distinct subsequences of string s that equal string t.
expected_approach: dp[i][j] = number of ways s[0..i-1] contains t[0..j-1] as subsequence. If s[i-1]==t[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j] (use or skip). Else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]. O(m*n) time and space.

text: Find the diameter of a binary tree (longest path between any two nodes).
expected_approach: DFS: at each node compute height of left and right subtrees. Diameter through this node = left_height + right_height. Update global max. Return 1 + max(left, right) as height to parent. O(n) time, O(h) space.

text: In a binary tree, find maximum sum path between any two leaf nodes.
expected_approach: Post-order: at each node, get max path sum from each subtree to leaf. Path through node = left_max + right_max + node.val. Update global answer. Return node.val + max(left, right) to parent. Must handle null children carefully. O(n) time.

text: House Robber on a tree: maximize sum of selected nodes where no two adjacent nodes can be selected.
expected_approach: For each node, dp returns pair (rob_this, skip_this). rob_this = node.val + skip_left + skip_right. skip_this = max(rob_left, skip_left) + max(rob_right, skip_right). Post-order traversal. O(n) time, O(h) space.

text: Find the number of paths of exactly k edges between two vertices in a graph.
expected_approach: DP with state (vertex, steps_remaining). Matrix exponentiation: adj_matrix^k gives paths of length k between all pairs. O(V^3 log k) with matrix exponentiation. Naive DP is O(k * V * E).

text: Find the longest path in a DAG.
expected_approach: Topological sort the DAG. Process in topological order: dp[u] = max over all incoming edges (v,u) of dp[v] + weight(v,u). Initialize all dp to 0 (or -infinity for strictly longest). O(V+E) time. Note: longest path in general graph is NP-hard.

text: Travelling Salesman Problem: find minimum cost Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities exactly once.
expected_approach: dp[mask][i] = min cost to visit cities in mask, ending at city i. Transition: dp[mask|(1<<j)][j] = min(dp[mask][j] + dist[j][next]) for unvisited cities. Answer: min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i] + dist[i][0]) for all i. O(2^n * n^2) time, O(2^n * n) space.

text: Given n jobs and n workers where each worker can do certain jobs, find assignment minimizing total cost.
expected_approach: dp[mask] = min cost to assign jobs in mask to first popcount(mask) workers. For each mask, try all unassigned jobs for current worker. O(2^n * n) time. Assignment problem — Hungarian algorithm gives O(n^3) but bitmask DP is cleaner for small n.

text: Count numbers from 1 to n that don't contain digit 4.
expected_approach: Digit DP: process number digit by digit. State: (position, tight_constraint, started). tight=true means we're still bounded by n's digits. At each position, choose digit 0-9 (or up to current digit of n if tight). Exclude 4. O(digits * 2 * 2) states, O(10) transitions.

text: Count integers in range [l, r] where sum of digits equals k.
expected_approach: f(r, k) - f(l-1, k) where f counts from 0 to x with digit sum k. Digit DP: state (position, remaining_sum, tight). At each position try all valid digits. Memoize states. O(digits * k * 2) states. O(10) transitions per state.

text: Find minimum number of meeting rooms required to schedule all meetings.
expected_approach: Sort meetings by start time. Use min-heap of end times. For each meeting: if heap top (earliest ending) <= current start, reuse that room (pop). Add current end to heap. Heap size at end = minimum rooms needed. O(n log n).

text: Given a set of intervals, insert a new interval and merge if necessary.
expected_approach: Three phases: collect intervals entirely before new interval (end < new_start), merge overlapping intervals (update new_start = min, new_end = max while overlap), collect remaining intervals. O(n) time after initial sort assumption.

text: Find the maximum profit from stock with cooldown (can't buy day after sell).
expected_approach: States: hold (have stock), sold (just sold, in cooldown), rest (no stock, not cooldown). hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1]-price). sold[i] = hold[i-1]+price. rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], sold[i-1]). Answer: max(sold[n], rest[n]). O(n) time, O(1) space.

text: Find the length of the longest chain of pairs where second element of previous < first of next.
expected_approach: Sort pairs by second element (end). Greedy: select pair if its start > end of last selected pair. This is equivalent to activity selection. O(n log n) sort + O(n) greedy. DP also works: O(n^2) with binary search optimization O(n log n).

text: Rotate an n×n matrix 90 degrees clockwise in-place.
expected_approach: Two steps: (1) Transpose the matrix (swap arr[i][j] with arr[j][i] for i<j). (2) Reverse each row. Both O(n^2) time, O(1) space. For counter-clockwise: reverse each column then transpose, or transpose then reverse each column.

text: Set entire row and column to zero if any element in a matrix is zero.
expected_approach: Use first row and first column as markers (with extra flag for first row/column themselves). Scan matrix: if cell is 0, mark its row (first column of that row) and column (first row of that column) as 0. Apply markers. O(m*n) time, O(1) space.

text: Traverse a matrix in spiral order.
expected_approach: Maintain top, bottom, left, right boundaries. Traverse right along top, down along right, left along bottom (if top!=bottom), up along left (if left!=right). Shrink boundaries after each direction. O(m*n) time.

text: Given a binary matrix, find the distance of each cell from the nearest 0 (BFS multi-source).
expected_approach: Multi-source BFS: enqueue all 0-cells with distance 0. BFS outward, distance of each 1-cell = distance when first reached. Since BFS explores level by level, first reach is minimum distance. O(m*n) time and space.

text: Find all prime factors of a number efficiently.
expected_approach: Trial division up to sqrt(n): for each i from 2 to sqrt(n), while i divides n, add i to factors and divide n by i. If n>1 after loop, n itself is a prime factor. O(sqrt(n)) time. Sieve of smallest prime factors for multiple queries: O(n log log n) preprocessing.

text: Compute nCr (combinations) modulo a prime p efficiently.
expected_approach: Precompute factorials and inverse factorials modulo p using Fermat's little theorem: inv(a) = a^(p-2) mod p (since p is prime). nCr = fact[n] * inv_fact[r] * inv_fact[n-r] mod p. O(n) preprocessing, O(1) per query. Lucas theorem for large n.

text: Given a number n, find all divisors of n efficiently.
expected_approach: Iterate from 1 to sqrt(n). For each i that divides n, add both i and n/i to divisors (handle perfect squares). Sort divisors. O(sqrt(n)) time. Count of divisors: for each prime factor p^a in factorization, multiply count by (a+1).

text: Find the number of trailing zeros in n! (factorial).
expected_approach: Count factors of 5 in n! (there are always more factors of 2). Count = floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ... (until term is 0). Each term counts multiples of increasing powers of 5. O(log_5(n)) time.
